coredata.dc:title: Radiation-convective heat transfer and performance analysis of a parallel-plates duct direct absorption solar heat collection system
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Chao-Hua
Liu, Yu-Xin
Zhang, Li-Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Solar photo-thermal conversion and solar heat collection are hot topics today. However, which solar collection mode is the best? The question has puzzled scholars for many years. In this study, a direct absorption solar heat collection (DASC) duct system was investigated. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were selected as the heat absorption medium. Different absorption modes were clarified. It is found that the ratio of the radiation penetration depth of nanofluids to duct depth (duct penetration ratio, α) plays a key role in the duct heat transfer and system performance. Totally three solar heat collection modes can be classified depending on the value of α: volume absorption (partial volume 0<α<1 or whole volume α = 1), surface absorption (upper α → 0 or bottom α → ∞) and hybrid absorption for α > 1. Of them, the whole volume absorption has the best performance when α = 1, while the hybrid performs the best when the penetration ratio is not unity. Surprisingly, a higher fluid absorption coefficient does not necessarily lead to a higher photo-thermal performance. The extreme high fluid absorption coefficients (upper surface mode) are inferior to extreme low absorption coefficients (bottom surface mode). Of the different modes, the total Nusselt numbers in the whole volume absorption mode are 2–3 times higher than those in the bottom surface absorption mode. For solar heat collection, convective heat collection is better than standstill collection, and a certain flow velocity should be ensured to have a satisfactory efficiency. The findings were experimentally verified.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar photo-thermal conversion and solar heat collection are hot topics today. However, which solar collection mode is the best? The question has puzzled scholars for many years. In this study, a direct absorption solar heat collection (DASC) duct system was investigated. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were selected as the heat absorption medium. Different absorption modes were clarified. It is found that the ratio of the radiation penetration depth of nanofluids to duct depth (duct penetration ratio, α) plays a key role in the duct heat transfer and system performance. Totally three solar heat collection modes can be classified depending on the value of α: volume absorption (partial volume 0<α<1 or whole volume α = 1), surface absorption (upper α → 0 or bottom α → ∞) and hybrid absorption for α > 1. Of them, the whole volume absorption has the best performance when α = 1, while the hybrid performs the best when the penetration ratio is not unity. Surprisingly, a higher fluid absorption coefficient does not necessarily lead to a higher photo-thermal performance. The extreme high fluid absorption coefficients (upper surface mode) are inferior to extreme low absorption coefficients (bottom surface mode). Of the different modes, the total Nusselt numbers in the whole volume absorption mode are 2–3 times higher than those in the bottom surface absorption mode. For solar heat collection, convective heat collection is better than standstill collection, and a certain flow velocity should be ensured to have a satisfactory efficiency. The findings were experimentally verified.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets generated during the current study are available from the corresponding author on request, Lizhi Zhang (Lzzhang@scut.edu.cn).

ce:section.ce:para: Till date, global population growth and economic development have led to a sharp increase in the demand for energy [1]. However, the consumption of traditional energy resources brings lots of austere environment and social problems [2,3]. Thus, sustainable energy production and application have become a major issue in our society today. With rich resources and least environmental impacts, solar energy utilization offers a promising and sustainable solution to the energy crisis [4]. At present, the main solar technologies can be categorized into photochemical, photovoltaic and photo-thermal [5] processes. Due to the close connection with people’s daily life, the photo-thermal technology has always been the research focus of scholars. The conventional solar thermal systems are always designed with black or spectrally selective surfaces. The solar radiations need to heat the absorbed surface firstly and then part of the heat is transferred to the working fluid by conduction and convection [6]. Most notably, large amount of the heat losses would be resulted in the heat transfer stage because of the large temperature difference between the solar absorbing surfaces and the ambient. It was reported that the heat losses can be up to 40 % of the incident solar energy [7]. Besides, the thermal resistance at the interface between the absorbed surface and the working fluid also greatly limits the efficiency of heat transfer [8,9].

In view of these problems, to obtain higher absorption performance, the solar collector should enhance the absorption from the sun, increase the heat transfer to the fluid and decrease heat losses to the surrounding environment. In 1970s, Chuang firstly presented the DASC (direct absorption solar heat collection) duct to absorb the solar energy directly by the heat transfer fluid (HFTs) [10]. The DASC duct shows more unitary transmittance and absorbance, which leads to enhancement on the solar absorption efficiency within the fluid volume [11]. As yet, the design details and the research progresses of DASC ducts have been introduced in quantities of studies [12–16]. It can be learned in these studies that the most commonly used HFTs include water, ethylene glycol (EG) and oil [17]. The so-called nanofluids used in the DASC duct are defined as the suspensions of nanoparticles, of which the particle sizes are ranging from 1 to 100 nm [18]. Studies also have shown that the absorption capability of nanofluids was much higher than that of base fluids over most range of the solar spectrum. This is mainly because that nanofluids have special optical properties and good thermal properties [19]. Generally, the nanoparticles used to prepare the nanofluids involve metals, nonmetal as well as their compounds [20,21]. The metallic materials have the effect of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), thus improving the photo-thermal efficiency of nanofluids [22]. Wang et al. reported a new Fe3O4/H2O nanofluids, achieving a photo-thermal conversion efficiency of 70.2 % [23]. Yang and Li et al. tested CuO/paraffin nanofluids and found that CuO was a good photo-thermal material [24]. It is noted that TiN also shows similar LSPR effect as noble metals. Ishii et al. used TiN/H2O nanofluids as solar heat transducer and proved that TiN had high sunlight absorption efficiency [25]. However, due to the inherent instability and rapid settling of nanoparticles, there were many drawbacks in the early designs of DASC ducts with large nanoparticles, such as clogging, erosion and abrasion of pipes and pumps [26]. With the decreases in particle sizes, the impact of Brownian agitation for the nanoparticles in the fluid medium overcomes that of the gravitational settling, which makes the nanofluids more stable [27].

With the advantages in wide distribution and low price, carbonaceous materials have always played a key role in the development of modern science and technology [28]. In DASC ducts, carbon nanotubes and graphene are the commonly used and the most promising materials for photo-thermal conversion [29,30]. However, the nanofluids prepared from these materials need lots of surfactant and considerable time expenses [31,32]. That is to say, the cost will be impressive when this type of nanofluids is applied in DASC ducts on an industrial scale. Therefore, it is imminent to find the appropriate materials to prepare the cheap and stable nanofluids. CQDs, as novel zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon nanomaterials, usually consist of crystalline amorphous materials [33]. With the advantages of good water-solubility, low toxicity, easy synthesis and high and broad absorption efficiency in the visible and near infrared light range, which accounts for 93 % of the total solar energy [34–37], it seems to offer a feasible solution to the above concerns. Sedaghat et al. found that CQDs and EG could be miscible well and the thermo-physical properties of the base fluid were also increased a lot [38]. Seifikar and Azizian et al. conducted a study on polyethylene glycol-200 (PEG-200) based on CQDs nanofluids. The results showed that the CQDs nanofluids had good photo-thermal conversion properties [39], thus providing a promising solution for solar heat collection. Overall, the main literatures involved in the researches of DASC ducts in this paper have been classified in Table 1 .

It can be found that most of these studies were confined to the feasibility of new materials in DASC ducts. Duct level flow and heat transfer analysis and system-scale performance analysis with these novel materials in DASC ducts are scarce. In fact, a real DASC system also comprises of circulating flows, heat storage tanks, in addition to the volume absorption duct itself. These components are closely interacted and they will influence the solar absorption efficiency finally. When implemented in engineering, the solar absorption is usually performed in parallel-plates ducts where nanofluid flows while collecting solar heat. Besides, the boundary conditions are different from those of the uniform wall temperature and uniform heat flux, which were the common boundary conditions assumed. The photo-thermal conversion and heat transfer properties would be quite different from ideal parallel-plates ducts [40,41]. Consequently, it is necessary to clarify the flow and radiation-convective heat transfer characteristics of the DASC ducts in solar heat collecting, as well as their coupling feature with heat storage systems, which would lay the foundation for system optimization in the future.

On the other hand, it is believed that the higher the solar light absorption coefficient is, the higher the photo-thermal conversion performance is. However, under what condition is the conclusion valid? There is currently no answer. Further, it should be noted that in a real system, the hybrid absorption is more common because both the fluid and the bottom surface would absorb solar heat and heat the fluid simultaneously, if the sunlight could penetrate the duct. So that happens in this mode? What are the boundaries between the volume absorption and surface absorption? There has been no mention to these questions, let alone strict mathematical definitions.

Answering above questions, in this study, a mathematical model governing the surface and volume absorption and radiation-convective heat transfer in a parallel-plates DASC duct, as well as the whole system performance, was established and numerically solved. Stable CQDs/EG nanofluids were prepared and used to validate the model. The flow and heat transfer properties in the duct for solar absorption were analyzed. The influences of operating parameters on the system performances were investigated. The above three questions were clarified after a system-scale analysis. The results could provide experimental and theoretical basis for future engineering practices.

Fig. 1 shows the diagram of the solar energy collecting and storage system. The system consists of a DASC parallel-plates duct, a heat storage water tank with a coil heat exchanger inside and a peristaltic pump to drive the nanofluids circuit for heat exchange. A mixer was installed inside the water tank to well mix the temperature of the water. Besides, to obtain a stable irradiance. In order to learn the thermal performances of the system, temperature sensors were installed along the circuit as indicated. The collected solar energy by the DASC duct was stored in the water tank. This system achieved the dynamic collection of solar energy by the coupling of the DASC duct with a storage tank, so heat collection would be more efficiently performed.

The raw materials used in the experiment included the citric acid purchased from Shanghai RunJie chemical reagents Co., Ltd; the urea purchased from FuChen chemical reagents Co., Ltd; the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) purchased from Shanghai LingFeng chemical reagents Co., Ltd. The ethanol and ethylene glycol were purchased from Tianjin FuYu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd.

The CQDs materials were prepared based on the method introduced in Bao et al [42]. Then the nanofluids used in this study were prepared by the two-step method. Firstly, ethylene glycol (EG) was used as the base fluid of the nanofluids. Then, a certain amount of nanoparticles was dispersed in the base fluid. Then it was treated for 0.5 h under the ultra-sonic bath to get homogeneously dispersed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and calculation of material properties

ce:section.ce:para: An ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Perkin Elemer, Lambda-950) was used to test the transmittance of the sample nanofluids. A transmission electron microscope (Japanese Electronics, JEM-2100) was used to observe the morphologies of the CQDs nanoparticles. An ellipsometer (Horiba, Uvisel Plus) was used to measure the complex refractive index of nanoparticles. A nanoparticle analyzer (Horiba, SZ-100Z) was used to conduct the DLS test. Fig. 2 shows the optical test results of the nanofluids and nanoparticles. Fig. 2a shows the extinction coefficients for the nanofluids of various concentrations. Fig. 2b and c are the DLS test results and TEM test results respectively.

It is clearly seen from Fig. 2a that CQDs/EG nanofluids exhibit higher extinctions than those of the pure EG in the entire wavelength range tested, which means that the light absorption property of the base fluid is significantly increased when the CQDs nanoparticles are added. Besides, from Fig. 2b and c, it can be found that the average diameter of CQDs is very small, almost 8 nm. The diameters of nanoparticles in nanofluids are also very small. The cumulative distribution of nanoparticles whose diameters are smaller than 15 nm can reach 85.6 %, which indicates CQDs/EG nanofluids have excellent stability in dispersion. In the following study, the performance of photo-thermal conversion is evaluated based on these tested material properties.

The photo-thermal conversion performance of the nanofluids also depends on its thermo-physical properties. For the density of the nanofluids, it can be determined based on the physical principle of mixture rule, that is [27]:

The differential scanning calorimeter (TA, Q2000) was used to evaluate the specific heats of nanoparticles. The Helium hydrometer (Builder, Td2200) was used to measure the density of nanoparticles. Table 2 lists the physical properties of the related materials.

Fig. 3 a shows the real set up of the experimental system. A solar simulator (Zhongke Microenergy, CEM-sol8100) was used to substitute the sun. For convenience, in test, a rectangular duct with an aspect ratio of 5 was used to simulate the parallel-plates DASC duct, whose dimensions are shown in Fig. 3b. After validation, in simulations followed, real parallel-plates duct is modeled. The top glass cover was made of low-reflectance Borosilicate (Pyrex) glass. The bottom surface and the side walls of the DASC duct were made up of the polypropylene (PP). To decrease heat losses, Styrofoam was used to insulate the outer and bottom surfaces of the DASC duct. PT100 sensors with the accuracy of ± 0.5 % were used to measure the temperature. The capacity of the tank was set to 2 L. The emissive power for the solar simulator was from 600-1200 W/m2. Table 3 lists the main parameters of the experimental devices.

The photo-thermal conversion experiments were performed in a laboratory with constant temperature of 25℃ and humidity of 70 %. Before the experiment, the pump was operated for a moment to ensure that the balanced temperatures for all the units. Then, the solar simulator was turned on and the DASC duct was used to collect the solar energy. When the system was stable, the experimental data was collected and transferred to the control computer by serial communication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluid flow and heat transfer model of the DASC duct
ce:section.ce:para: The parallel-plates DASC duct is shown in Fig. 4 schematically with heat transfer processes. Nanofluids flow along the duct while collecting solar heat from the radiations penetrating through the top glass cover so the heated fluid could transport the collected heat to the tank where it is stored. Incident solar radiations penetrate through the upper glass cover, and travel through the duct depth while releasing radiation heat to the nanofluid. When the emissive power is in an excess, the rest of the emissive power would be absorbed and diffusively reflected by the lower PP surface. The fluid itself would exchange heat with the upper and the lower surfaces in a radiation-convection manner. The lower PP surface is insulated and the upper glass cover dissipates heat to ambient by natural convection. For a parallel-plates duct, due to symmetry, a two-dimensional duct flow is considered.

When the fluid could absorb all the solar irradiation during its penetration, it is an exact volume absorption. When the fluid is transparent, all the irradiation would be absorbed by the bottom plate and the heat will be then released to fluid by convection. In this case, it is actually a surface solar collector. The bottom surface could be painted with solar absorption paintings to enhance the solar-thermal conversion. In most cases, both the volume absorption and the surface absorption co-exist when the fluid absorption during the penetration is incomplete, this is called the hybrid absorption.

It the solar radiation reaches the bottom surface, the boundary conditions is reflected by Eq.(35) in the following section with the inclusion of solar heat flux.

The energy equation of the DASC duct Eq. (8) contains an internal heat source produced by the incident radiation. The radiative transfer equation (RTE) in participating media is usually employed to describe the spectral attenuation of radiation within the semitransparent nanofluids depth [45]. ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance indices for the DASC system
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the performance of the system, the η si (the instantaneous solar heat collecting efficiency of the system) and η sm (the average solar heat collecting efficiency of the system) are defined respectively as:

where dT w is the temperature difference of the water in the tank (K) during interval (dt, s), ΔT w is the total temperature difference of the water in the tank (K) during the whole test time (t total, s; 9hrs in the study) and A D is the solar collector area (m2).

The above models for the conjugate problem with duct, the upper cover, the lower plate, as well as the heat storage system, are solved numerically. The flow chart of the system simulation is shown in Fig. 5 , which is performed by a simultaneous execution of MATLAB and COMSOL tools. The detailed solution procedures are introduced as follows:
(1)
Input initial parameters (i = 0): the optical and thermo-physical properties of nanoparticles and base fluid; the ratio of refractive indexes (m); the average diameter of nanoparticles (D); the mass flow rate of nanofluids in the DASC duct (
m ̇
D
); the volume concentration of nanoparticles (f v); the initial temperature (T w 0) of the water in the heat storage tank; the thermal physical properties of the plates. Set Δt as the calculation time step of the system simulation.
(2)
Solve the optical model of nanofluids and obtain the spectral extinction coefficients (β nf), absorption coefficients (κ nf) and scattering coefficients (σ snf), by Eqs.(36)-(45).
(3)
Solve the thermo-physical model of nanofluids and obtain the density, specific heat, thermal conductivity and viscosity of nanofluids, by Eqs.(1)-(4).
(4)
Assume an initial value of the temperature difference between the water temperature exiting the coil heat exchanger and the water in the tank as ΔT.
(5)
Set the inlet nanofluids temperature (T i(gue) i) as T w i + ΔT.
(6)
According to the initial environmental parameters, solve the flow and heat transfer model for the DASC duct and obtain the outlet nanofluids temperature (T o i), by Eqs.(5)-(21).
(7)
Solve the mathematical model for the coil heat exchanger and obtain its outlet temperature (T i(cal) i), which is also the inlet temperature to the solar collector.
(8)
Calculate the new temperature difference (ΔT’ = T i(cal) i - T w i). If the difference between ΔT and ΔT’ is less than 10-3, then set the present water temperature in the tank as T w i and go to (9). If not, set ΔT = (ΔT + ΔT’)/2 and continue to solve the model for the coil heat exchanger.
(9)
Solve the energy balance equation of the water tank and calculate the temperature (T w(cal) i).
(10)
If iΔt = t end, exit the program. Otherwise, go to the next time step (i = i + 1) and return to (4) until iΔt = t end.
(11)
Calculate the solar heat collecting efficiency of the system, by Eqs.(63)-(64).

It is noticed that the model of the DASC duct is developed based on the RTE and N-S equations. Combining with the corresponding boundary conditions, the discrete ordinates method and the finite element method are used to solve the RTE and N-S equations by the COMSOL tool respectively. The discretization of the computational domain is conducted by the built-in unstructured meshing algorithm of COMSOL. The Mie scattering model and the energy storage tank model are calculated by MATLAB tool. During the simulation process, calling the calculated results of the DASC duct from COMSOL by using MATLAB, the whole system operating simulation can be completed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the Mie scattering model
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the Mie scattering model for CQDs, based on Eq. (45), the spectral absorbances of 200 ppm CQDs/EG nanofluids were measured and calculated respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , it can be seen that the calculated results are generally in good agreement with the experimental results and the relative deviation of the peak values for the calculated and experimental results is only 6.25 %. It can also be found that the calculated peak value appears between 500 and 600 nm while the tested peak value appears between 600 and 650 nm. That is mainly due to the existence of some larger nanoparticles in the nanofluids, which leads to a red shift for the absorbance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the model for the system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to fully validate the mathematical model for the whole system, based on the above established experimental system, CQDs/EG nanofluids of 200 ppm was selected to conduct the photo-thermal conversion experiments. Besides, the experiments under different flow rates were carried out. In the experiments, the incident radiation of the solar simulator was set as 1000 W/m2 and the height of the nanofluids was 2 cm. The simulations were performed for the same geometry of the rectangular duct as used in the test. Fig. 7 shows the experimentally obtained and simulated results under different working conditions. The largest relative deviation between the numerical and experimental results is within 3.2 %. It indicates that the established mathematical model in this study is accurate and reliable to describe the real operation of the system within the error range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of thermodynamic performances between the DASC duct and common parallel-plates duct
ce:section.ce:para: Parallel-plates duct is a common structure for heat exchangers, which has been extensively studied in classical heat transfer textbooks. The parallel-plates DASC duct here as depicted in Fig. 4 is different from a common duct in that it absorbs solar radiation volumetrically. In this section, the thermodynamic performances of the parallel-plates DASC duct and the common duct under typical working conditions will be solved and compared. Their structural and working conditions are compared in Table 4 . For the common duct, a uniform heat flux boundary condition is used to substitute the incident heat flux condition for the DASC duct at the upper surface.

Figs. 8 and 9
are the flow and temperature distributions for the DASC duct and the common parallel-plates duct respectively. Data on the x-y mid-planes are plotted. As shown in Fig. 8, it can be found that there are almost no differences in flow distributions between the DASC duct and common duct.

As shown in Fig. 9, it can be seen that the temperature distributions in the two ducts are different. The temperature in the DASC duct is significantly greater than that in the common parallel-plates duct. Besides, it can be seen that the temperature differences in the DASC duct are also larger than those in the common duct. These changes show that the temperature boundary layer in the DASC duct becomes thicker, compared with those of the common duct. This is because that nanofluids are typical participating medias. The incident radiations are absorbed, scattered or emitted by nanofluids. The absorbed radiations will generate the heat sources inside the nanofluids, which greatly promotes the heat transfer inside the nanofluids, making the temperature inside higher. It is more efficient in heat transfer compared to surface heating. The incoming radiation is depleted before reaching the bottom surface.

The distribution of the internal heat source Q r of nanofluids at different positions can be solved by solving the RTE. Fig. 10 shows the vertical distribution of the internal heat source at the center of x axis. It can be found that the maximum heat source appears adjacent to the upper surface with a value of 5.7 × 105 W/m3. As the incident radiations penetrate through the nanofluids, the value of the internal heat source decreases sharply. For example, at the depth of 3 mm, the value of the internal heat source becomes less than 1 × 105 W/m3. With increasing depth, the value of the internal heat source tends to become zero. It implicates that a large depth of the nanofluid is not involved in solar heat absorption, as can also be seen from the temperature fields in Fig. 9.

Fig. 11 shows the temperature of the two surfaces of the glass covers for the DASC duct and the common parallel-plates duct. It can be seen that the temperature of the upper surface for the glass cover is higher than that of the lower surface for the common duct. However, the temperature of the upper surface for the glass cover of the DASC duct is slightly smaller than that of the lower surface. This is due to the differences in heat transfer mechanism. For the common duct, the boundary condition at the upper surface of the glass cover is a uniform heat flux. Thus, the glass cover is heated firstly, then the heated wall will further heat the nanofluids. For the DASC duct, incident radiations behave like internal heat sources inside the nanofluids. The nanofluids will be heated firstly. Then the heat of fluids will be transferred to the wall. Due to the differences in heat transfer mechanism, the convective heat losses of the DASC will also be smaller than that of the latter.

Fig. 12 shows the outlet temperature distributions of the DASC duct and the parallel-plates duct under the typical working conditions. It can be found that the outlet temperature of the DASC duct is higher than that of the common duct in general. However, the temperature adjacent to the upper surface of the DASC duct is lower than that at similar positions of the common duct. This is because the convective heat losses between the DASC duct and the environment are substantial here, leading to temperature decrease near the glass surface.

To evaluate flow characteristics, local (fRe) along the flow direction is calculated. For the laminar flow in the parallel-plates duct, (fRe) along the flow direction reduces asymptotically from a high value near the entrance of the duct to a low value at the end of the entry length. The fully developed (fRe) is 96 [54] for a parallel-plates duct. Fig. 13 shows the calculated results of local (fRe) for the DASC duct and the common duct. As can be seen, there is almost no difference in (fRe) between these two ducts. In the inlet section, the values of (fRe) are much larger compared with those in the fully developed section. When the flow of the fluid tends to be stable gradually, the values of (fRe) tend to be convergent. The (fRe) in the fully developed section is about 95.9, which is close to the theoretical value. Overall, incident radiations do not change the fluid flow characteristics of the DASC duct.

In order to investigate the effects of incident radiations on heat transfer quantitatively, local Nusselt number (Nu) along the flow direction is calculated, as shown in Fig. 14 . Both convective, radiative, and total Nusselt numbers are plotted. The Nusselt number of the bottom plate tends to be zero because no solar radiation reaches the bottom surface and it absorbs and releases no heat. For ideal laminar flow in the common parallel-plates duct, under the working condition of one side uniform heat flux and the other adiabatic, the fully developed Nu c is 5.38 [55]. It can be found in Fig. 14 that the local Nu c of the common duct tends to 5.38 along the flow direction gradually. Besides, it can also be found that there are great differences in the local Nu of the DASC duct. Due to the role of incident radiations, the effect of radiative heat transfer occupies a huge share in the DASC duct. Thus, the local Nu r of the DASC duct is far greater than Nu c. Simultaneously, it is observed that due to the existence of the non-linear internal heat source Q r, there are downward trends in the local Nu c,upper of the DASC duct in the outlet region, which illustrates that there is no thermally fully developed regions in the DASC duct. This is consistent with the conclusions of Yu [56]. From above analysis, it is concluded that the DASC duct is quite different in Nusselt numbers from the common duct. Therefore, it is wrong to use the Nusselt data from a common duct in estimating the convective heat losses from the DASC ducts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Various working modes of the DASC duct
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the concentration of nanofluids used in the experimental setup is 200 ppm. Under this condition, according to Eq. (54), the duct penetration ratio α of the duct is about 0.17, which means that radiation depleted quickly and the solar heat absorption and releasing by the bottom plate can be overlooked. If the nanofluids concentration is not very large, the duct penetration ratio may be higher than 1, the role of substrate will be substantial. The surplus solar radiation will be absorbed by the bottom surface and it will then be released to the fluid by convective heating. The local Nu c both at the upper and the bottom surfaces of the duct are substantial, but with different values. The duct penetration ratio, which can be adjusted by concentration directly, is a determining factor. In the following, to investigate the effects, the duct penetration ratio is set to 0.01, 1 and 20 respectively. Their effects on flow and heat transfer performances are evaluated. Two substrate properties are considered: 0.2 and 0.95 for bottom surface absorption coefficient, respectively.

Fig. 15 shows temperature distributions in the DASC ducts with different duct penetration ratios and substrate absorption coefficients. In general, it can be found that when the duct penetration ratio is much larger than 1 (Fig. 15a, and b), solar radiation could penetrate the whole duct depth with negligible absorption by the fluid. Fluid is like a transparent medium now. Most of the radiation can reach the bottom surface. After absorption of the solar radiation, the bottom surface would heat the fluid then by convection. Obviously, this is very similar to a surface solar collector. The higher the absorption coefficient is, the higher the solar energy absorbed is, and the more effective of solar collection is. This can be categorized as surface absorption.

When the duct penetration ratio is as appropriate as 1, solar radiation is just used up when it reaches the bottom surface (Fig. 15c and d). All the solar energy is absorbed by the fluid volume directly. This mode is exactly the whole volume direct volume absorption solar collection. In this case, because the bottom surface seldom participates in heat collection, increases in substrate absorption coefficient have limited effects in heat collection.

The nanofluid concentrations are further increased to decrease the penetration ratio. When the duct penetration ratio becomes very small as 0.01 (Fig. 15e), the fluid would have very high absorption ability to irradiation so that the solar radiation can travel only a small depth. They will be used up quickly. In an extreme case when the penetration ratio is infinitely large, the solar radiation would be absorbed by the fluid upper surface as soon as it meets the surface. The heated surface would then heat the bulk fluid directly by convection. This mode is in fact another surface absorption collection. The upper surface absorption is similar to the bottom surface absorption, but the heat losses from the upper surface in this case are much higher than the heat losses from the bottom which is well insulated. So the upper surface absorption mode is much inferior than the bottom surface absorption. As shown in Fig. 15, outlet temperature in Fig. 15b (32.61 ℃) is higher than that in Fig. 15e (31.06 ℃). So a too high fluid solar absorption coefficient has no good for volume collection. It is contrary to the common belief. Since the bottom surface does not participate in heat absorption and transfer, increasing its absorption coefficient has no effect. In Fig. 15e, the variation in absorption coefficient of the bottom plate has no influences in the solar absorption performance of the duct.

In summary, when the duct penetration ratio is infinite (α ⟶ ∞), the DASC duct would be in surface absorption mode. When the duct penetration ratio is 0 (α = 0), the DASC duct would also be in surface absorption mode, but by a hypothetical upper surface formed by the nanofluids particles. When the penetration ratio is high (1<α<∞), some fraction of radiation can reach the bottom surface, so now both the bottom surface and the fluid volume participate in the solar absorption, which can be called a hybrid absorption. When the penetration ratio is small (0<α<1), the radiation is used up during its trip to the bottom surface. Consequently, only a part of the duct volume participates in the heat absorption, this can be called the partial volume absorption. Only when the duct penetration ratio is equal to 1 (α = 1), it is in an exact whole volume absorption mode where all the duct volume (depth) participates in the solar radiation absorption and photo-thermal conversion. This is called the whole volume absorption. It can be guessed that this mode is the most efficient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of penetration ratios and absorption modes on Nusselt numbers
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effects of the duct penetration ratios, as well as the absorption modes on the performances of the DASC ducts quantitatively, Nu c, Nu r and Nu t for the DASC ducts under different absorption modes are calculated. The duct penetration ratios are set to 0.01, 1, 20 and 200 respectively. The absorption coefficient of the bottom plate is set to 0.95.

When the duct penetration ratio α = 200 and 20, the collector can be regarded as in a bottom surface absorption mode, although it’s true when α = 20, the volume absorption still contributes to 17 % of the overall heat gain. For larger penetration ratios, almost all the incident radiations are absorbed by the bottom plate. As can be seen in Fig. 16 a and b, the Nusselt numbers for the upper surface and the radiation, Nu c,upper and Nu r, are far smaller than the Nusselt numbers for the bottom surface, Nu c,bottom. Hence, the bottom surface Nu c,bottom is nearly equal to the total Nusselt number, Nu t. Since the radiative heat transfer is weak, and the glass cover cannot be heated fully, the convective heat flux to the glass is also weak. As a result, the upper surface Nusselt numbers are small and negative, meaning heat is dissipating from it.

When the duct penetration ratio α = 1, the collector can be regarded as in a whole volume absorption mode. Most of the incident radiations are absorbed by the nanofluids, and the bottom plate has little effects. As can be seen in Fig. 16c, both the bottom and the upper surface Nusselt numbers are rather smaller. Since most of the solar heat is absorbed by nanofluids volume, the total Nusselt number is largely composed by the radiative Nusselt number. The total and radiative Nusselt numbers are much larger than the bottom surface Nusselt numbers in the previous cases. Comparing Fig. 16c to Fig. 16a, the total Nusselt numbers in the whole volume absorption mode are 2–3 times higher than those in the bottom surface absorption mode. All these proves that the volume absorption is the most efficient.

If the concentration is increased further, the incident radiations can hardly penetrate the duct. The collector will be in a partial volume absorption mode, or even in an upper surface absorption mode, as indicated in Fig. 16d for a duct penetration ratio of 0.01. It can be regarded as the upper surface absorption mode. The solar radiation will be absorbed (partially reflected) by a hypothetical surface formed by the nanofluids particles. The total Nusselt numbers are comparable, even inferior to those in a bottom surface absorption mode. This mode is much inferior to the volume absorption mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of absorption mode on system efficiencies
ce:section.ce:para: Solar heat is absorbed and transported by the duct to the water tank where it is stored finally. The duct penetration ratio and the consequent absorption mode have clear impacts on the system performance. Their effects on the system performances are discussed in the following. The duct penetration ratios are selected as 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5 and 20 respectively, by the adjustment in nanofluids concentrations. Other operating parameters are fixed as above.

Figs. 17 and 18
show the effects of the penetration ratios on the instantaneous and mean solar heat collecting efficiencies of the system. It can be found that the instantaneous performance of the system decreases with time lapses. As the system operates, water tank temperature increases, and heat losses rises. So the efficiency decreases with time. Among the different penetration ratios, the α = 5 has the highest efficiency, but its deviation from α = 1 is negligible, indicating both the whole volume absorption and the hybrid absorption modes perform the best. However further increases in the penetration ratio would have adverse effects. For the hybrid absorption mode, the bottom plate absorption coefficient also plays a key role. As indicated by Fig. 18, when α = 5, but χ plate = 0.2, the performance deteriorates. This is because when the penetration ratio is above 1.0, some radiation can reach the bottom plate. The duct is now in a hybrid mode. If the plate absorption coefficient is satisfactory, the reached radiation could be absorbed and transported to the fluid efficiently, resulting a higher performance. In contrary, if the bottom surface absorption coefficient is low as 0.2, the reached radiation would be reflected and scattered, and the absorbed heat would be limited. As a consequence, the system performance will deteriorate in the hybrid mode with lower substrate absorption coefficient. In this case, the optimum duct penetration ratio around 1.

In order to experimentally confirm the above concepts, the nanofluids with different concentrations were prepared and used to conduct the photo-thermal conversion tests with the test rig depicted in Fig. 3a. In the experiments, the duct penetration ratios are set as 0.1, 1, and 3.3 (base fluid) respectively. The experimentally obtained average efficiencies are shown in Fig. 18. It can be found that the experimental results are very close to the calculated results. The trends of the results also agree with the calculated curves, which mean that the above analysis are correct.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of flow rates on system efficiencies
ce:section.ce:para:
To fully explore the impacts of the varying nanofluids flow rates on the system performance, different flow rates for nanofluids are simulated while other operating conditions are kept unchanged. In this section, the duct penetration ratios are kept as 0.01, 1 and 20 respectively. Figs. 19 and 20
are average solar heat collecting efficiencies with the bottom plate absorption coefficients of 0.2 and 0.95 respectively.

It is found that for both the high and low absorption coefficient substrates, the efficiency of the system increases as the flow rate increases. The reason is that when circulating faster, instant mean temperature of the circulating fluid will be lower and more heat can be exchanged between the heat sources (either the nanoparticles or the bottom surfaces) and the fluid. Solar heat absorption power will be higher. However, when the flow rates are higher than 25 mL/min, the penalty from increased resistance will be higher. The heat losses from the system to surroundings will also be higher after the system is heated up. Then the overall influences from risen flow rates would become negligible. So the effects of flow rates on system efficiencies are small. In contrast, the absorption mode has a great role to play. A whole volume absorption (α = 1) always performs the best. A bottom surface absorption (α = 20) with high bottom surface absorption coefficient (χ plate = 0.95) exceeds the whole volume absorption only when the flow rate is low. This implicates that for solar heat collection, convective heat collection is better than standstill collection, and a certain flow velocity should be ensured to have a satisfactory efficiency. In other words, the key in solar collection is to let the nanofluids move.

A comprehensive analysis of the solar heat collection system was performed, from the system point of view and duct radiation-convective heat transfer. A mathematical model for the whole system of DASC system including the absorption and collecting parallel-plates duct, the heat storage, and circulating system, was built and validated by experimental data with CQDs/EG nanofluids. The effects of operating parameters, focusing on the duct penetration ratio, on system performance were investigated. Following conclusions can be given:
(1)
The established mathematical model to describe the system operation is accurate and reliable. The largest relative deviation between the numerical and experimental results is within 3.2 %.
(2)
Compared with the common parallel-plates duct, the friction factors change little. However, the Nusselt numbers for the upper surface, the bottom surface, the radiation, and the overall duct, show tremendous differences. Previous assumption for the boundary conditions of a uniform heat flux or a uniform temperature boundary are wrong. It is erroneous to use the Nusselt data from a common duct in estimating the convective heat losses from the DASC ducts.
(3)
The duct penetration ratio is a determining factor for DASC duct. Three modes can be classified by its value. The absorption of solar radiation can be volume (partial volume or whole volume), surface (upper surface or bottom surface) and hybrid modes, depending on the penetration ratio. It is a misunderstanding that a higher light absorptivity necessarily leads to a higher solar collection efficiency. For the DASC duct in this study, the optimum penetration ratio is around 1 when a whole volume absorption occurs.
(4)
The total Nusselt numbers in the whole volume absorption mode are 2–3 times higher than those in the bottom surface absorption mode. For solar heat collection, the key is to let the nanofluids move. Convective heat collection is better than standstill collection, and a certain flow velocity should be ensured to have a satisfactory efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the total error in experimental measurements is usually expressed in terms of systematic (bias) and random (precision) errors. The systematic errors are considered as a constant during the measurement, thus the precision error for a test is mainly determined by the frequency of the repeated measurements from an independent parameter and the resulted standard deviation. The formula for the total error can be expressed as:
